summary: Physics-grounded industrial AI model for energy operations.
editor_note: Industrial AI demos often fail because models ignore physics, plant constraints, and sensor context. Applied Computing's Orbital product is more specific: it learns from DCS, historian, LIMS, and plant data, then monitors sensors, forecasts outcomes, finds set-points, and flags equipment warnings. Unlike a dashboard, it is framed as a plant model for operational decisions. The risk is scaling beyond expert-led deployments.

product_evidence.claim: The official site says Orbital monitors thousands of sensors, forecasts outcomes, finds optimal set-points, and informs early equipment warnings in real time.
The official site describes Orbital connecting to DCS, historians, and LIMS while constraining learned plant behavior with physics.
The funding source says Orbital combines time-series data, physics-based models, and language models to flag anomalies, investigate causes, and model proposed fixes.

market_context.primary_user: Energy operators, refinery teams, petrochemical plant managers, process engineers, and industrial operations leaders.
market_context.differentiation: Applied Computing is packaging plant-wide sensor data, engineering documents, and physics constraints into an operational model for set-points and anomaly investigation.
market_context.why_now: Energy operators are under pressure to squeeze more reliability, safety, and yield from existing assets while experienced process engineers are hard to replace.

risks.claim: The falsifiable risk is whether Orbital can scale from expert-supported deployments to repeatable plant onboarding across different assets, process histories, and data quality levels.
risks.basis: VentureDex assessment from the official product site, demo link, careers page, and Series A coverage.
